text: The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the fundus of the gallbladder, revealing the infundibulum and Hartmann's pouch. The surgical field shows significant adipose tissue, requiring the omentum to be pushed down to bring the gallbladder into view.
The surgeon begins the dissection by removing the peritoneal covering from the sides of the gallbladder, which appears to have a stone lodged in its neck.
The surgeon removes the fat and peritoneal cover from the gallbladder area, stripping it off to approach the cystic duct and cystic artery.
The surgeon exposes the naked gallbladder, showing the cystic duct joining the gallbladder to the biliary ducts. The cystic artery is clearly visible as it comes into view.

The surgeon mobilizes the gallbladder from its position on the right lobe of the liver and the gallbladder bed, aiming to achieve the Critical View of Safety (CVS) by clearly visualizing the cystic artery and cystic duct.
The surgeon mobilizes the gallbladder, lifting it slightly above its original position, with approximately one-third of the gallbladder now mobilized.

The cystic artery is clearly visible, surrounded by small vessels and fibro-fatty tissue that is being removed from both sides to achieve the critical view of safety. The surgeon uses a tooth grasper to hold the fundus of the gallbladder, pushing it back to facilitate anterior dissection. Posterior dissection is also being performed.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully separating the gallbladder from the liver tissue.
The surgeon displays the critical view of safety, clearly showing the cystic duct, cystic artery, and liver bed, with the liver visible in the background.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, posterior branch of the cystic artery, main cystic artery, common hepatic duct, and common bile duct. Clips are applied to these structures to prevent bleeding and bile leakage during removal of the gallbladder.
The surgeon clips both the posterior and anterior branches of the cystic artery simultaneously, securing them to prevent bleeding during the cholestectomy procedure.

Two clips are applied to the cystic artery, leaving the anterior and posterior branches intact. The surgeon carefully applies a clip to the cystic duct, ensuring it is not placed too tightly to avoid tenting of the common hepatic duct and common bile duct. The clip is pressed to expel fluid from the tissue, preventing slippage.

The surgeon uses scissors to cut the cystic duct, which is now free, while noting small vessels that may be lymphatics. The posterior branch of the cystic artery is clearly visible going to the gallbladder. The surgeon prepares to remove the gallbladder from its fossa using a cautery hook.
The surgeon removes the peritoneum and fascia covering small vessels from the gallbladder bed, addressing any bleeding from veins and small arteries by cauterizing them.

The surgeon has clipped the vessels connected to the gallbladder and is now coagulating any remaining connections with the liver vessels. The gallbladder is ready for extraction, and suction is being applied to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon observes a small amount of blood coming from the gallbladder fossa and identifies a vessel requiring coagulation. Using a broad spatula, the surgeon applies coagulation to stop the bleeding, then performs suction to check for any other bleeding points.
The surgeon completes the removal of the gallbladder from its fossa, marking the end of the surgical procedure.
